When you run the RFA function, after 2 minutes, the system shows a warning message in the application log that the RFC executor is taking longer than expected, and the progress loading is no longer displayed on the UI.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

However, the posting continues to run in the background. To monitor progress, you can check the logs on the results screen for real-time updates. The Y-Table is updated as soon as a posting result is available, and upon each refresh of the results screen, you will be able to see the updated table (the number of rows will increase) and the posting details in the FS_PER_MSG_TEXT_ column.

Why this is great for you:

No more waiting for the function to complete the run to get the information about the progress. You'll still have all the necessary details even if there's a delay.

So, to conclude: We’ve fine-tuned the RFA to make everything faster and more user-friendly. Postbacks now complete more quickly, and the improved logging in the Y-Table streamlines tracking, enabling smoother processes and efficient handling of large data volumes. Whether you’re troubleshooting or processing data, these updates are designed to make your work easier.

Restriction ‌‌ ⚠️
The enhancements mentioned in this blog post do not apply to the following RFA types:

  • Post Journal Entries for Group Reporting
  • JavaScript
  • Python

In addition to these primary changes, we have generally improved the message logs to make them more user-friendly and informative. We have also added two new RFA function types – Finance Accounts Payable Items and Finance Accounts Receivable Items. Check out our What's New blog post for more information about them.

I hope you find these updates helpful! If you have any questions or comments, feel free to post them in the SAP Community Questions section. To read more about the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Standard Model, use the PaPMCloudSM tag in your search.

Thanks for reading my first blog post—here’s to many more! 😊

entry.title: Recurring Process and Multi-Selection of Activities in Process Scheduler
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!  

Today marks the last blogpost for the SAP Profitability and Performance Management (SAP PaPM) Cloud – Standard Model for the year 2024.  

As we have introduced the Process Scheduler during Q3 of 2022 in this blogpost, Process Scheduler: Execution made easy by Scheduling a “Job” in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud, we are pleased to announce the new enhancements of the Process Scheduler.  

Previously, a scheduled job in Process Scheduler could run a specific activity in a process template or process instance at a specific date and time. But great news everyone! Due to the requests of our customers, I am delighted to announce that our Process Scheduler can now handle recurring processes to which you can schedule a job that will run activities every day, every hour, every month, and so on. Yes! You’ve read it right. A job can now handle multiple activities in a process template or process instance that will run sequentially. 

How will it work? Let’s dive right in.  

In adding a new Job or copying through Job From, there are 2 new fields in “Add process schedule” dialogue, namely Repeat Interval and End time.  

1. Repeat Interval – In this field, you can specify the interval for the job to repeat. You can enter a number followed by a time unit such as 2 days, 1 week, 3 months, etc. For instance, if you enter “2 days”, the job will be triggered every 2 days at the scheduled launch time. Only numbers and English time units (for example, days, weeks, months) are currently being accepted.  

Note: This feature uses the SAP Job Scheduling Service. For detailed instructions on entering the repeat interval correctly, see Human-Readable Date Formats section in SAP Job Scheduling Service Cloud documentation.  

2. End time – This field will be enabled once “Repeat Interval” is maintained. In this section, you can set an end date and time to when the recurring runs will stop. If this is left blank, it will also be accepted and will run recurrently that will only stop by deleting the job created.  

3. Activities - This field is where we can select 1 or more Execution Activities in a process template or process instance that we would choose to run sequentially based on the order of selection made.  

After confirmation, the information about the job created will be displayed on top of the list in the left-hand side panel. Let’s highlight the column header “Next planned launch”, which was previously named “Planned launch”, that will show when the next run recurrence is happening. In our case, this is the first run. Also, there is a new column heading introduced here, which is the “Repeat Interval”.  

Now let’s focus on the right-hand side panel.  

Validation of the execution status of the recurring processes can be checked under “Runs” located in the right-hand side panel.  

Runs – Displays information about each job run. You can see the sequence number of the currently displayed job run in relation to a total number of scheduled job runs (for example 1/2 or 2/2), and you can switch between them using the arrows next to the job count. 

Now, I will show you an example of how it works. In this example, we will schedule a job that will run 2 activities every 5 minutes with a total of 4 Runs.  

After confirming, the system will create a job, which will appear at the top of the list of scheduled jobs with the status “SCHEDULED”. The panel on the right will show more detailed information about the job run.  

After the 1st successful run, the next planned launch will be changed to the next scheduled recurrence and the state will still be in “SCHEDULED” status. The Runs will create another page below the scheduled Job run page (2/2). 

After the 2nd successful run, the next planned launch will be changed to the next scheduled recurrence and the state will still be in “SCHEDULED” status. The Runs will create another page below the scheduled job run 2/3.  

After the 3rd successful run, the next planned launch will be changed to the next scheduled recurrence and the state will still be in “SCHEDULED” status. The Runs will create another page below the scheduled job run.   

After the 4th successful run, since it will be the last recurrence, the state of the job will now be set to “COMPLETED” and all the runs are now set to ”COMPLETED”. 

And there you have it. Like and share this blog post with your colleagues who may be interested in this new feature. Watch out for future enhancements and improvements of the Process Scheduler. This information is also available in our Application Help

Thank you and Happy holidays!

entry.title: PaPM Early Exit Check
